Home»Sport»Paris Olympics 2024»Paris Olympics 2024: Size of the task facing Team SA

Size matters. We’d be well advised to keep that in mind when it comes to assessing Team South Africa’s Paris Olympics prospects.

Size matters, it really does. As does context. So, as we tick off the days to the start of theParis Olympics 2024we’d bewell advised to keep that in mindwhen it comes to assessingTeam South Africa’sprospects.

For the latest Paris Olympics 2024 news, bookmarkThe South African website’sdedicated section

A visual reminder of what they, and every other country, is up against at the Games can be seen almost immediately after exiting the building. As you look out a bedroom window you’ll see Serbia’s flags. That’s where the likes of Novak Djokovic will stay. Around the corner you’ll see a whole eight-storey apartment block adorned in the colours of Team USA.

There’s no missing them. Loud music blares from a speaker in their gym. Or, correctly referenced, their weights room. The USA claims to be “bigger and better”. Simply on size, it’s hard to argue that won’t be the case at Paris 2024 either.

At Tokyo 2020 (held three years ago), Team USA topped the medals table with 113 – 39 of them being gold, They were followed by China (89 medals), Japan, Great Britain and ROC (Russian Olympic Committee). France were eighth in Tokyo, but expect them to move up the table as hosts who have thrown resources into the cause as hosts.

Without putting a number on it, Team SA will be looking to better their tally ofthreemedals from Tokyo 2020. And they should achieve that, although we’re not comparing apples with apples when it comes to “us” and “them”.

That’s not to say that there aren’t giants among the Team SA athletes who are represented in 19 different codes. I’m thinking that there are a handful of names who wouldn’t be out of place in Team USA colours. They include track athletes Akani Simbine and Wayde van Niekerk, swimmers Tatjana Smith and Pieter Coetze (and despite his age, perhaps Chad Le Clos), surfer Jordy Smith, and even our golfers.

Plus, the men’s Sevens rugby players will fancy their chances if they come up against Team USA.

However, the sheer weight of resources counts more than isolated talent. The visual of Team USA’s building within the village brings home the enormity of the task though. They’re being represented by some 600 athletes coming from a country that has a 330-million population. Much of their squad is fully professional, whereas much of Team SA’s members have day (and night) jobs and have to fit in their sports careers either side of work.

Which is why every medal Team SA wins is celebrated with gusto. After a while the USA will be counting their golds more than they will silver or bronze. After all, their 39 golds were down on the 46 and 48 of the two previous Games’. Only twice in the last 10 Olympics’ stretching back to 1984 in Los Angeles have the USA not hit 100 medals, with their lowest being 93 at Sydney 2000.

Therefore, a betting person would probably lay the line at 40 gold medals for them in Paris. So, when judging one’s own country, one needs to get the full picture in terms of size and resources. And the context is: should Team USA not reach 110 medals, or 40 golds, then they will feel that they have under-achieved.

Gary Lemke is reporting live from Paris for the Team SA website for the duration of the Paris Olympics 2024
